verse_specific_anchors: na hi prapaśyāmi mamāpanudyād — quoted verbatim by the engineering director in the concrete scenario as the diagnostic-limit declaration

even maximal external success would not dispel the state — anchored on rājyaṁ surāṇām api ca ādhipatyam (sovereignty over the gods), rendered as 'the architecture redesign succeeding spectacularly, the headcount doubling, the promotion arriving, the company going public'
the diagnostic limit is in the framing, not the toolkit — anchored on the chapter's pivot from external-acquisition-as-remedy to a different kind of knowledge (which begins at 2.11)
verdict_reason: I tested against three candidate verses where 'I've tried everything' might be claimed: BG 1.46 (Arjuna sits down on the chariot), BG 2.4 (Arjuna asks how to fight teachers), BG 6.34 (the mind is restless and difficult to control). Each has different anchors: 1.46 anchors on physical collapse and weapon-dropping; 2.4 anchors on the duty-vs-respect contradiction; 6.34 anchors on the mind's restlessness specifically. This engineering analog explicitly anchors on the *exhaustion of tools-and-interventions* (not the exhaustion of arguments or the failure of physical effort) and on the claim that even maximal success would not address the underlying state. The 'rājyaṁ surāṇām api' force is preserved in the engineering ladder. Verse-specific. PASS.
